This motion addresses the deficiencies in the plaintiff's pleading under Rule 12. The plaintiff has not adequately alleged facts sufficient to support each element of the claim as required for plausibility. Under applicable federal and state standards, a complaint must provide more than labels and conclusions to survive scrutiny. The defendant therefore requests dismissal with prejudice where appropriate. The plaintiff's factual allegations are threadbare in several respects and do not permit the court to draw a reasonable inference in the plaintiff's favor on key elements. The defendant incorporates by reference the arguments set forth in prior briefing and addenda without repeating them in full here. For the foregoing reasons, and for the reasons stated in the accompanying memorandum of law, the motion should be granted. Section 01 elaborates jurisdictional points. Section 02 addresses standing. Section 03 addresses failure to state a claim. Section 04 addresses remedies. Section 05 addresses costs. Section 06 addresses preservation. Section 07 addresses waiver. Section 08 addresses mootness. Section 09 addresses ripeness. Section 10 addresses abstention. The court should dismiss because the complaint fails under Iqbal and Twombly as applied in this circuit. The plaintiff cannot cure deficiencies merely by re-labeling conclusions as facts. The defendant reserves all rights. The defendant requests oral argument if the court deems it helpful. Respectfully submitted. Additional grounds appear in the record excerpted in the appendix references. The plaintiff's theory of liability alternates between negligence and contract without coherent factual support for either. The defendant repeats that dismissal is appropriate under the applicable pleading standard. The complaint's conclusory allegations do not nudge claims across the line from conceivable to plausible. The defendant incorporates the factual background from the notice of removal where applicable. The parties dispute material facts not suitable for resolution on a motion to dismiss limited to the face of the complaint. Even accepting the complaint as true, the plaintiff still fails to state relief. The defendant preserves objections to any amendment that merely rephrases the same defects. The motion is supported by the declarations filed concurrently. The defendant requests costs under applicable rules. The plaintiff's opposition, if any, should address the deficiencies identified above with specificity rather than general denials.
